rescue_reasoning: Although the sub-statement is about staffing ("Create a Quality Assurance Verifier position"), it is explicitly in service of the Building Performance Ordinance ("support the guidelines and applicability of the Building Performance Ordinance"). The parent statement makes the mitigation outcome explicit: "retrofit existing buildings to reduce emissions from energy use in buildings." Therefore the primary gate failure (missing mitigation outcome token in the sub-statement) is due to overly strict token matching; the measure is still climate-mitigation relevant as implementation support for an ordinance that reduces building energy use/emissions.
policy_statement: Create a Quality Assurance Verifier position to support the guidelines and applicability of the Building Performance Ordinance.
mechanism_description: Create a quality assurance verifier role to support the guidelines and applicability of a building performance ordinance, helping improve building energy performance and reduce energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building operations/retrofits → ↓ energy demand (and/or ↓ fossil fuel use) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Owners/operators of existing buildings measure and disclose energy use (benchmarking), optimize controls and maintenance (retuning/commissioning), and invest in efficiency upgrades and equipment replacements (retrofits; sometimes including electrification set).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved building envelope, HVAC efficiency, controls, and end-use equipment reduce delivered energy needed for the same services (heating/cooling/lighting/process loads); when electrification is included, on-site combustion is reduced and emissions shift to (often cleaner over time) grid electricity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower site and/or source energy use reduces combustion of fossil fuels and upstream electricity generation emissions, decreasing CO₂ (and sometimes CH₄/N₂O) emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption (and associated fuel/power generation) leading to lower greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: These policies primarily reduce energy use in existing buildings through measurement, optimization, and upgrades, which reduces GHG emissions from on-site fuel use and electricity generation.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: Although efficiency also fits “Resource Efficiency” conceptually, the mechanism’s main climate outcome is explicitly emissions reduction via reduced energy-related combustion; it is not primarily about adapting to hazards or enhancing ecosystems.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific ordinance focuses mainly on resilience/thermal safety (e.g., passive survivability) with minimal energy/emissions impact, it could lean Adaptation—but that would be a different mechanism than “energy_use_reduction.” If the policy is purely electrification without net energy reduction, it still often reduces emissions depending on grid mix; however, the stated mechanism here centers on energy-use reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that benchmarks/retunes/retrofits to cut building energy waste shares the same dominant effect—lower energy demand leading to lower emissions—so Mitigation remains the correct primary category for the mechanism family.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether an individual policy is best tagged as “efficiency,” “electrification,” “disclosure,” or “performance standard,” and the strength of enforcement or equity design (e.g., fee-in-lieu funding) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some building performance ordinances include electrification or carbon-intensity compliance paths; if electrification increases electricity use while reducing on-site fossil fuel use, the dominant impact may be “fuel switching” rather than “energy reduction,” but both still primarily serve mitigation. If benchmarking is purely informational with no action/enforcement, near-term realized mitigation can be weaker, though the intended dominant pathway remains emissions reduction.
key_indicators: "Create a Quality Assurance Verifier position"; "support the guidelines and applicability"; "Building Performance Ordinance"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as “planning/strategy” (capacity building) rather than a mandate; however, because it is explicitly to support implementation of an ordinance (a regulatory framework), it is classified as regulatory-mandate with enabling directness.
